stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) has characteristic polynomial \( (\lambda-[[c]])^2(\lambda-[[d]]) \). What is the algebraic multiplicity of the eigenvalue \( \lambda=[[c]] \) (its multiplicity as a root of the characteristic polynomial)?
solution: Since \( \lambda=[[c]] \) appears as a root of the characteristic polynomial \( (\lambda-[[c]])^2(\lambda-[[d]]) \) with exponent 2, its algebraic multiplicity is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This under-counts; \( (\lambda-[[c]]) \) appears SQUARED in the factorization, not just once.
The exponent of \( (\lambda-[[c]]) \) in the factorization is 2, not 3; the other factor \( (\lambda-[[d]]) \) belongs to a different eigenvalue.
An eigenvalue that actually appears as a root cannot have algebraic multiplicity 0.
This exceeds the total degree of the characteristic polynomial contributed by \( \lambda=[[c]] \).

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has characteristic polynomial \( (\lambda-[[c]])^3 \). What is the algebraic multiplicity of the eigenvalue \( \lambda=[[c]] \)?
solution: Since \( \lambda=[[c]] \) is the ONLY root of the characteristic polynomial \( (\lambda-[[c]])^3 \), appearing with exponent 3, its algebraic multiplicity is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This under-counts; the factor \( (\lambda-[[c]]) \) appears with exponent 3, not 1.
This under-counts; the factor \( (\lambda-[[c]]) \) appears with exponent 3, not 2.
An eigenvalue that appears as the ONLY root cannot have algebraic multiplicity 0.
This exceeds the degree of the characteristic polynomial, which is only 3.

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[k]] \\ 0 & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as its only eigenvalue, with algebraic multiplicity 2. Its geometric multiplicity is \( 2 - \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \). What is the geometric multiplicity of \( \lambda=[[c]] \)?
solution: \( A-[[c]]I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & [[k]] \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \) has rank 1 (since \( [[k]] \ne 0 \)), so the geometric multiplicity is \( 2-1 = [[answer]] \): only ONE independent eigenvector, even though the algebraic multiplicity is 2. This is why \( A \) is NOT diagonalizable.
distractor_rationales: This equals the algebraic multiplicity, but the geometric multiplicity here falls SHORT of it (that's exactly what makes \( A \) defective).
The eigenspace of an actual eigenvalue can never be trivial (0-dimensional).
The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue can never exceed its algebraic multiplicity, which is only 2 here.
A multiplicity (a dimension) can never be negative.

stem: The diagonal matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as its only eigenvalue, with algebraic multiplicity 2. What is the geometric multiplicity of \( \lambda=[[c]] \)?
solution: \( A - [[c]]I \) is the \( 2\times2 \) zero matrix (rank 0), so the geometric multiplicity is \( 2-0 = [[answer]] \): it matches the algebraic multiplicity exactly, so \( A \) (already diagonal) is diagonalizable.
distractor_rationales: This falls short of the algebraic multiplicity, but here \( A-[[c]]I \) is the ZERO matrix, so every vector is an eigenvector and the full multiplicity is achieved.
The eigenspace of an actual eigenvalue can never be trivial (0-dimensional).
The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue can never exceed its algebraic multiplicity, which is only 2 here.
A multiplicity (a dimension) can never be negative.

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[k1]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[k2]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as its only eigenvalue, with algebraic multiplicity 3, and geometric multiplicity 1 (a single Jordan chain). What is the defect (algebraic multiplicity minus geometric multiplicity) of \( \lambda=[[c]] \)?
solution: The algebraic multiplicity is 3 and the geometric multiplicity is 1, so the defect is \( 3-1 = [[answer]] \); this defect (the shortfall in independent eigenvectors) is exactly why \( A \) is not diagonalizable.
distractor_rationales: A defect of 0 would mean \( A \) is diagonalizable, but here the geometric multiplicity falls strictly short of the algebraic one.
This is the geometric multiplicity itself, not the DIFFERENCE between the two multiplicities.
This is the algebraic multiplicity itself, not the difference between the two multiplicities.
A defect (a difference of two multiplicities with algebraic \( \ge \) geometric) can never be negative.
